questions.questionText: The nerve most commonly injured in fracture of the shaft of humerus is:
questions.explanation: The radial nerve lies in the spiral (radial) groove on the posterior surface of the humeral shaft. Mid-shaft fractures can injure it, causing wrist drop (loss of wrist/finger extension). Ulnar nerve is injured at the medial epicondyle, axillary nerve at the surgical neck, and median nerve at the supracondylar region. Memory aid: 'ARM' — ARM shaft = Radial nerve. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Upper Limb, Brachial Plexus.

questions.questionText: Erb's palsy (upper brachial plexus injury) involves which nerve roots?
questions.explanation: Erb-Duchenne palsy results from injury to upper trunk (C5-C6) of brachial plexus, commonly due to forceful widening of head-shoulder angle during birth. It causes 'waiter's tip' position — arm adducted, medially rotated, forearm pronated. C8-T1 injury causes Klumpke's palsy. C7 alone would affect wrist extension partially. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brachial Plexus.

questions.questionText: A newborn has claw hand deformity with Horner's syndrome. The injury involves:
questions.explanation: Klumpke's palsy involves the lower trunk (C8-T1). It causes claw hand (paralysis of intrinsic hand muscles). When T1 sympathetic fibers are also involved, Horner's syndrome (miosis, ptosis, anhidrosis, enophthalmos) occurs. Erb's palsy (C5-C6) causes waiter's tip position, not claw hand. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brachial Plexus.

questions.questionText: The artery palpated in the floor of the anatomical snuffbox is:
questions.explanation: The radial artery crosses the floor of the anatomical snuffbox (bounded by tendons of extensor pollicis longus medially and abductor pollicis longus/extensor pollicis brevis laterally). The scaphoid bone forms the floor — hence scaphoid fractures can damage the radial artery here. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Wrist and Hand.

questions.questionText: A 30-year-old male cannot extend his wrist after a humeral shaft fracture. On examination, he has loss of sensation over the first dorsal web space. The nerve injured is:
questions.explanation: Wrist drop (loss of extension at wrist and fingers) + loss of sensation over first dorsal web space (autonomous zone of radial nerve via superficial branch) = Radial nerve injury. Median nerve injury causes loss of thenar muscles/sensation over lateral 3.5 fingers palmarly. Ulnar nerve injury causes claw hand. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Radial Nerve.

questions.questionText: Winging of scapula is caused by paralysis of which muscle?
questions.explanation: Serratus anterior (supplied by long thoracic nerve of Bell, C5-C7) holds the scapula against the chest wall. Its paralysis causes winging of the scapula, especially when pushing against a wall. Trapezius paralysis (spinal accessory nerve) causes drooping of shoulder, not classic winging. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Muscles of Shoulder.

questions.questionText: The nerve passing through the carpal tunnel is:
questions.explanation: The carpal tunnel contains: median nerve + 4 tendons of FDP + 4 tendons of FDS + tendon of FPL (total 9 tendons + 1 nerve). The ulnar nerve passes through Guyon's canal (superficial to flexor retinaculum). Anterior interosseous nerve branches before the carpal tunnel. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Wrist.

questions.questionText: A patient presents with inability to flex the IP joint of the thumb and DIP joints of the index and middle fingers. The nerve injured is:
questions.explanation: Anterior interosseous nerve (branch of median nerve) supplies FPL, lateral half of FDP (to index and middle fingers), and pronator quadratus. It is a pure motor nerve — no sensory loss. This distinguishes it from median nerve injury at the elbow which would also cause sensory loss. Posterior interosseous is a branch of radial nerve. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Forearm Nerves.

questions.questionText: The rotator cuff muscle that inserts on the lesser tubercle of humerus is:
questions.explanation: Subscapularis is the only rotator cuff muscle inserting on the lesser tubercle. Memory aid: 'SItS' — Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, teres minor on greater tubercle (Superior to Inferior), Subscapularis on lesser tubercle. Subscapularis is the most powerful medial rotator of the shoulder. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Shoulder Joint.

questions.questionText: Fracture of the surgical neck of humerus most commonly damages which nerve?
questions.explanation: The axillary nerve winds around the surgical neck of humerus through the quadrangular space. Fracture here causes deltoid paralysis (loss of arm abduction beyond 15°) and loss of sensation over the 'regimental badge area' (lateral aspect of upper arm). Radial nerve is damaged in shaft fractures. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Shoulder Region.

questions.questionText: Saturday night palsy is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Saturday night palsy = radial nerve compression in the spiral groove (e.g., arm draped over a chair while intoxicated). It causes wrist drop due to paralysis of wrist and finger extensors. Claw hand = ulnar nerve. Ape thumb = median nerve (low). Hand of benediction = median nerve (high). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Radial Nerve.

questions.questionText: The dermatome of the thumb is supplied by:
questions.explanation: C6 dermatome covers the thumb and lateral forearm. Memory aid for upper limb dermatomes: C5 = lateral arm (regimental badge area), C6 = thumb, C7 = middle finger, C8 = little finger, T1 = medial forearm, T2 = medial arm. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Dermatomes.

questions.questionText: A child presents with cubitus valgus and tardy ulnar nerve palsy. The most likely cause is non-union of fracture of:
questions.explanation: Non-union of lateral condyle fracture → lateral condylar growth continues while medial stops → progressive cubitus valgus → ulnar nerve stretching over medial epicondyle → tardy ulnar nerve palsy (develops months to years later). Supracondylar fractures cause Volkmann's ischemic contracture (anterior interosseous syndrome). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Elbow Joint.

questions.questionText: Froment's sign is positive in lesion of which nerve?
questions.explanation: Froment's sign: patient asked to hold paper between thumb and index finger — compensatory flexion of thumb IP joint (using FPL, supplied by AIN/median) occurs due to paralysis of adductor pollicis (ulnar nerve). This is a key clinical test for ulnar nerve palsy. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Hand Muscles.

questions.questionText: The most commonly fractured carpal bone is:
questions.explanation: Scaphoid is the most commonly fractured carpal bone, typically from a fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH). Blood supply enters distally → proximal fragment prone to avascular necrosis (AVN). Tenderness in anatomical snuffbox is the classical sign. Lunate is the most commonly dislocated carpal bone. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Carpal Bones.

questions.questionText: A 25-year-old patient with a stab wound in the posterior triangle of neck presents with inability to shrug the shoulder. The nerve injured is:
questions.explanation: Spinal accessory nerve (CN XI) traverses the posterior triangle superficially, making it vulnerable in neck injuries. It supplies trapezius (shrugging) and sternocleidomastoid. Long thoracic nerve damage causes winging of scapula. Dorsal scapular nerve supplies rhomboids. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Posterior Triangle.

questions.questionText: Hand of benediction is seen on attempting to make a fist due to injury of:
questions.explanation: Hand of benediction occurs with high median nerve lesion — on attempting to make a fist, index and middle fingers remain extended (due to loss of FDS and lateral FDP). The ring and little fingers can flex (medial FDP supplied by ulnar nerve). Do NOT confuse with 'claw hand' (ulnar nerve) which is at rest. EXAM TRAP: Benediction = active attempt; Claw = at rest. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Median Nerve.

questions.questionText: The muscle responsible for unlocking the knee joint is:
questions.explanation: Popliteus (supplied by tibial nerve) laterally rotates the femur on the fixed tibia, 'unlocking' the fully extended knee to initiate flexion. It also retracts the lateral meniscus during knee flexion. Memory aid: 'POPliteus POPs open the locked knee.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Knee Joint.

questions.questionText: The muscle NOT forming the rotator cuff is:
questions.explanation: Rotator cuff muscles: SITS — Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis. Teres major is NOT part of the rotator cuff. It is supplied by the lower subscapular nerve and acts as a medial rotator and adductor. Teres minor (axillary nerve) IS part of the rotator cuff. EXAM TRAP: Teres minor vs Teres major. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Shoulder.

questions.questionText: The primary abductor of the shoulder joint from 0° to 15° is:
questions.explanation: Supraspinatus initiates abduction (0-15°), deltoid continues 15-90°, and trapezius + serratus anterior (rotating scapula) take over from 90-180°. Supraspinatus is the most commonly torn rotator cuff tendon. It is tested by the 'empty can test' (Jobe's test). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Shoulder Movements.

questions.questionText: A patient with supracondylar fracture of humerus develops absent radial pulse, severe forearm pain, and pain on passive extension of fingers. The most likely complication is:
questions.explanation: This describes compartment syndrome leading to Volkmann's ischemic contracture — ischemia of forearm flexor muscles due to brachial artery injury/compression. The 5 P's: Pain (esp. on passive extension), Pulselessness, Pallor, Paralysis, Paresthesia. Supracondylar fracture is the MC fracture in children causing this. Treatment: urgent fasciotomy. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Anterior Compartment of Forearm.

questions.questionText: The nerve injured in Colles' fracture is:
questions.explanation: Colles' fracture = fracture of distal radius with dorsal displacement and angulation ('dinner fork deformity'). The median nerve is most commonly injured due to its close proximity to the distal radius. Acute carpal tunnel syndrome can result. Colles' fracture is caused by FOOSH in elderly (osteoporotic bone). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Wrist.

questions.questionText: The autonomous zone of the ulnar nerve is:
questions.explanation: Autonomous zone = area supplied exclusively by one nerve. Ulnar nerve = palmar surface of little finger tip. Median nerve = palmar surface of index finger tip. Radial nerve = first dorsal web space. These are key for clinical testing. EXAM TRAP: Do not confuse with 'total area of supply.' Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Cutaneous Innervation.

questions.questionText: The muscle that is the chief supinator when the elbow is fully flexed is:
questions.explanation: Biceps brachii is the most powerful supinator when the elbow is flexed at 90°. Supinator muscle acts in slow, unopposed supination regardless of elbow position. Brachioradialis brings forearm to mid-prone position. This is why we turn a screw with elbow flexed — to use biceps power. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Forearm Muscles.

questions.questionText: The structure NOT passing through the quadrangular space is:
questions.explanation: Quadrangular space contents: Axillary nerve + Posterior circumflex humeral artery. Boundaries: Superior — subscapularis/teres minor, Inferior — teres major, Medial — long head of triceps, Lateral — surgical neck of humerus. The anterior circumflex humeral artery does NOT pass through it. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Axilla.

questions.questionText: A 50-year-old woman presents with tingling and numbness in the lateral 3.5 fingers, worsening at night. Tinel's and Phalen's tests are positive. The diagnosis is:
questions.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome = median nerve compression under flexor retinaculum. Presents with numbness/tingling in lateral 3.5 fingers (median nerve territory), nocturnal symptoms (fluid redistribution), positive Tinel's (tapping over carpal tunnel) and Phalen's (wrist flexion for 60 seconds). MC in middle-aged women, hypothyroidism, pregnancy, RA. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Carpal Tunnel.

questions.questionText: The nerve supply of the rhomboid muscles is:
questions.explanation: Dorsal scapular nerve (C5) supplies rhomboid major, rhomboid minor, and levator scapulae. Long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior. Thoracodorsal nerve supplies latissimus dorsi. Suprascapular nerve supplies supraspinatus and infraspinatus. Memory aid: Dorsal scapular nerve → muscles on Dorsal surface of scapula (rhomboids). Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Brachial Plexus Branches.

questions.questionText: The most common site of deep vein thrombosis in the upper limb is:
questions.explanation: The axillary vein is the MC site for upper limb DVT (Paget-Schroetter syndrome — effort thrombosis), especially in athletes/after strenuous activity. The subclavian vein can also be involved (related to thoracic outlet). The cephalic vein drains into the axillary vein in the deltopectoral groove. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Upper Limb Veins.

questions.questionText: In a posterior dislocation of shoulder, the limb is held in:
questions.explanation: Posterior shoulder dislocation (rare, ~2% of dislocations): arm is adducted and internally rotated, and the patient cannot externally rotate the arm. It is classically associated with electric shock and epileptic seizures (massive internal rotator muscle contraction). Anterior dislocation (MC, 95%) shows abduction and external rotation. EXAM TRAP: Most exams ask about posterior dislocation associations. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Shoulder Joint.

questions.questionText: The nerve that hooks around the medial epicondyle behind the elbow is:
questions.explanation: The ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel (between the two heads of flexor carpi ulnaris). It is the most superficial nerve here — hence the 'funny bone' sensation when hitting the medial epicondyle. Cubital tunnel syndrome = ulnar nerve compression here. Reference: Gray's Anatomy — Elbow Region.
